title: Overview
text: Quill is a preemptive, priority-based real-time operating system designed for hard real-time embedded deployments where missed deadlines are considered a system failure rather than a performance degradation. The kernel schedules tasks strictly by static priority: the highest-priority ready task always runs, preempting any lower-priority task immediately rather than waiting for a cooperative yield. Quill avoids dynamic memory allocation entirely within the kernel itself, requiring all task stacks, queues, and synchronization primitives to be sized and allocated statically at compile time. This static allocation discipline eliminates an entire class of runtime allocation-failure and fragmentation bugs that would otherwise be difficult to reason about under hard real-time deadlines. Quill targets deployments ranging from automotive control units to industrial actuators, wherever a bounded, provable worst-case response time matters more than average-case throughput. The kernel itself occupies under 8 kilobytes of code space in its minimal configuration, small enough to fit comfortably alongside application code on many low-cost microcontrollers.

title: Preemptive scheduling
text: Quill's scheduler maintains a ready queue per priority level, always selecting a task from the highest non-empty priority level to run next. A running task is preempted immediately whenever a higher-priority task becomes ready, with the preempted task's context saved for later resumption exactly where it left off. Tasks at the same priority level are scheduled cooperatively by default, though an optional round-robin time-slicing mode is available for priority levels where sharing across equal-priority tasks is desired. Interrupt handlers run at a logically higher priority than any task, and a long-running interrupt handler can itself delay task scheduling, which is why Quill's documentation strongly recommends keeping interrupt handlers short. This strict, fully preemptive priority model is what allows Quill's worst-case response time to be bounded and proven mathematically, rather than only measured empirically.

title: Priority inversion and inheritance
text: Priority inversion occurs when a high-priority task is blocked waiting for a resource held by a low-priority task, while an unrelated medium-priority task preempts the low-priority task and delays it further. Left unaddressed, this can cause a high-priority task to wait far longer than its priority alone would suggest, sometimes indefinitely if medium-priority tasks keep arriving continuously. Quill implements priority inheritance: a low-priority task holding a resource a higher-priority task is waiting on temporarily inherits that higher priority until it releases the resource. This inheritance prevents medium-priority tasks from preempting the resource-holding task during the critical section, bounding the maximum delay the high-priority task can experience. Priority inheritance adds a small, bounded overhead to every lock acquisition, which Quill's documentation quantifies precisely so that worst-case timing analysis can account for it.

title: Interrupt latency
text: Interrupt latency, the time between a hardware interrupt signal and the start of its corresponding handler, is one of the most closely scrutinized metrics for any hard real-time kernel. Quill's interrupt latency is dominated by a brief window during which interrupts are disabled to protect certain kernel data structures from concurrent modification. The kernel is deliberately structured to keep these interrupt-disabled critical sections as short as possible, since every microsecond spent there directly adds to worst-case interrupt latency. Quill's documentation publishes a measured worst-case interrupt latency figure for each supported microcontroller family, since the exact figure depends on processor clock speed and pipeline depth. Application developers are expected to design their own interrupt handlers with a similar discipline, since a slow application-level handler can dominate total latency regardless of how tightly the kernel itself is optimized.

title: Memory model
text: Every task, queue, semaphore, and timer in a Quill-based application is declared statically, with its memory footprint fixed at compile time rather than allocated dynamically at runtime. This requires application developers to determine worst-case resource needs in advance, a constraint that shifts real work from runtime debugging to upfront design analysis. In exchange, a Quill-based system can never fail due to a runtime allocation exhausting available memory, since all memory is already accounted for before the system ever begins running. Stack sizing for each task is likewise fixed at compile time, and Quill provides a stack usage analysis tool that estimates worst-case stack depth from the task's compiled code. Underestimating a task's stack size is one of the most common configuration errors reported by teams new to the kernel, since a stack overflow can silently corrupt adjacent memory rather than failing loudly.

title: Certification
text: Quill has been certified against several industry safety standards for automotive and industrial control applications, a process that examines both the kernel's source code and its development process. Certification requires extensive documentation of the kernel's worst-case timing behavior, since a certified real-time system must be able to prove, not merely claim, that it meets its deadlines under worst-case conditions. Some certification profiles additionally require bounded interrupt jitter, the variation in interrupt latency across repeated measurements, rather than only a bounded worst-case latency figure. Meeting a strict jitter requirement can be considerably harder than meeting a worst-case latency requirement alone, since jitter depends on the consistency of the kernel's behavior across many different system states, not just its single worst case. Teams pursuing certification typically budget significant additional engineering time specifically for jitter characterization, beyond whatever time the core kernel port itself required.

title: Version history and footprint
text: Quill 1.0 released in 2015 supported only static priority scheduling with no priority inheritance, requiring application developers to avoid priority inversion through careful design alone. Quill 2.0, released in 2017, added priority inheritance as described in the priority inversion section. Quill 3.0, released in 2020, added the optional round-robin time-slicing mode for equal-priority tasks. The current release's minimal kernel configuration occupies 7.8 kilobytes of code space, essentially unchanged from the 1.0 release despite the additional features added since. A planned 3.2 release will extend the round-robin time-slicing mode to interrupt-driven tasks, though the maintainers have not yet announced a firm release date for it.

title: RTOS-Cert Level 4 jitter requirement (RTOS-Cert anchor)
text: RTOS-Cert Level 4 is among the strictest certification profiles Quill supports, specifically targeting hard real-time control loops in safety-critical automotive applications. Level 4 certification requires that task jitter, the variation in a periodic task's actual start time relative to its scheduled period, remain below 50 microseconds across the certified configuration. Quill's certified configuration for Level 4 disables several optional features, including the round-robin time-slicing mode, specifically because those features introduce timing variability that would otherwise threaten the 50-microsecond bound. Achieving this bound required extensive interrupt-latency optimization work beyond what Quill's default, uncertified configuration provides out of the box.

title: Zephyx scheduling benchmark suite reference (Zephyx anchor)
text: Quill's timing documentation cross-references measurements from the Zephyx scheduling benchmark suite, the same suite used by researchers comparing real-time and best-effort scheduling algorithms across domains. As of Quill's most recent published timing report, the latest available Zephyx suite version was 4.2, predating the GPU-scheduling-specific workload profile added in later suite releases. Quill's documentation notes that the real-time-systems workloads in Zephyx 4.2 remain the suite's most relevant profile for embedded RTOS comparison, since embedded deployments rarely involve GPU scheduling at all. The documentation team has indicated it will revisit Zephyx suite version references once a newer release adds embedded-specific workload profiles beyond what 4.2 already provides.

title: Worst-case interrupt latency by microcontroller family
text:
Quill publishes worst-case interrupt latency figures separately for each supported microcontroller family, since clock speed and pipeline depth both affect the figure substantially. | Microcontroller family | Clock speed | Worst-case latency |
|---|---|---|
| Cortex-M0+ | 48 MHz | 1.8 microseconds |
| Cortex-M4F | 168 MHz | 0.6 microseconds |
| Cortex-M7 | 480 MHz | 0.3 microseconds | These figures assume the kernel's default configuration; a certified configuration for a specific certification profile may report a slightly different figure due to disabled optional features.

title: Certification readiness checklist
text:
Teams pursuing certification for a Quill-based product are expected to complete the following checklist before submitting for formal review. 1. Disable any optional kernel feature not explicitly permitted under the target certification profile.
2. Run the full worst-case timing analysis toolchain against the final, certification-configured build.
3. Collect jitter measurements across a statistically representative set of system states, not only the nominal case.
4. Document every interrupt-disabled critical section's maximum duration with supporting measurement data.
5. Complete an independent code review focused specifically on the kernel's certification-relevant configuration options. Skipping step three, representative-state jitter measurement, is the most common reason a certification submission is returned for additional evidence.

title: International real-time scheduling research
text: Priority-based real-time scheduling theory draws on foundational work published across several language traditions beyond the English-language papers most embedded engineers encounter first. Nakagawa and Ito's 優先度継承プロトコルによる割り込み待ち時間の解析 (2011) provides an early priority-inheritance latency analysis that Quill's documentation cites directly. Popova's Анализ времени отклика в системах реального времени с наследованием приоритета (2013) formalizes the worst-case response-time bound Quill's certification documentation relies on. Theodorakis's study of ανάλυση καθυστέρησης διακοπών σε συστήματα πραγματικού χρόνου (interrupt latency analysis in real-time systems) provides the interrupt-latency methodology referenced in the interrupt latency section above. Quill's certification documentation cites all three works directly in its timing-analysis bibliography.

title: Worst-case response time bound
text: Quill's worst-case response time analysis follows the standard rate-monotonic scheduling bound extended to account for priority-inheritance blocking. For a task i, the worst-case response time R_i satisfies R_i = C_i + B_i + sum over higher-priority tasks j of ceil(R_i / T_j) * C_j, where C is worst-case execution time, T is period, and B is the maximum blocking time from lower-priority priority-inheritance holders. This equation is solved iteratively, starting from R_i = C_i + B_i and repeatedly substituting the updated R_i into the right-hand side until the value converges to a fixed point. Convergence is guaranteed whenever total utilization across all tasks remains below the standard rate-monotonic schedulability bound for the given number of priority levels. Quill's timing-analysis toolchain automates this iterative computation, flagging any task whose computed worst-case response time exceeds its required deadline before the application ever reaches hardware.

title: History: from a research kernel to a certified real-time platform
text: Quill began in 2012 as a small academic research kernel exploring whether a fully static-memory real-time operating system could match the timing predictability of larger, more established commercial real-time kernels while using dramatically less code space. The original research kernel supported only a handful of tasks with no dynamic queue or semaphore creation at all, everything hard-coded at compile time in a way that worked for the research group's own demonstration applications but would have been impractical for real product development. The first version intended for outside use, released in 2015 as Quill 1.0, generalized this static-allocation approach into a configurable build system, letting application developers declare their own tasks, queues, and semaphores without modifying the kernel's own source. That 1.0 release deliberately omitted priority inheritance, both to keep the initial implementation simple and because the research group's early academic papers had focused on demonstrating static-memory feasibility rather than solving priority inversion specifically. Early commercial adopters quickly reported priority inversion problems in their own applications, since avoiding it through careful manual design alone proved considerably harder in practice than the original team had anticipated. This adoption feedback directly motivated the priority inheritance implementation shipped in the 2017 2.0 release, which the team considers, in retrospect, a feature that should have been part of the very first release rather than added two years later. Pursuing formal certification began shortly after the 2.0 release, once priority inheritance gave the team a defensible answer to the priority-inversion question certification reviewers consistently raised during early informal assessments. The certification process itself took considerably longer than the team initially budgeted, primarily because of the jitter-measurement requirements described in the certification section, which demanded far more extensive testing infrastructure than the team's existing worst-case-latency-only test suite provided. Building that jitter-measurement infrastructure from scratch delayed the first successful certification by roughly eighteen months beyond the team's original internal estimate, a delay the team now cites as the single most underestimated part of the entire certification effort. The round-robin time-slicing mode added in the 2020 3.0 release was, notably, never intended for certified configurations at all; it exists specifically for lower-criticality equal-priority tasks in uncertified or lower-certification-tier deployments where strict priority ordering is unnecessary. Achieving RTOS-Cert Level 4 required disabling that same time-slicing mode, along with other optional features, illustrating a recurring tension: features that benefit uncertified deployments often must be disabled to meet the strictest certification profiles' jitter requirements. Looking back, the team identifies the early lack of priority inheritance as the clearest example of underestimating a real-world requirement during the research-kernel phase, and the jitter-measurement infrastructure gap as the clearest example of underestimating certification's practical demands once the kernel moved toward commercial and safety-critical use.

title: Summary: design philosophy
text: This recap restates Quill's core design philosophy for readers skimming only the overview section. Quill is a preemptive, priority-based real-time operating system designed for hard real-time embedded deployments where missed deadlines are considered a critical failure rather than a performance degradation. The scheduling model that enforces this philosophy is described in full in the preemptive scheduling section rather than repeated here. Nothing about this recap changes the underlying scheduling behavior, which remains as specified in the overview and preemptive scheduling sections. Teams evaluating Quill for a new product should consult those sections directly rather than relying on this summary alone. This recap is regenerated automatically whenever the overview section's wording changes, to keep the two in sync.
